title: Symmetry-Selective Stabilization of Charge-Density Wave in ScV$_6$Sn$_6$
authors: A. Korshunov, C. -Y. Lim, J. Corral-Sertal, G. Garbarino, D. Chernyshov, A. Rajapitamahuni, C. Yi, S. Roychowdhury, C. Shekhar, C. Felser, V. Pardo, Ella M. Schmidt, S. Blanco-Canosa
abstract: Charge-density-wave (CDW) order in kagome metals is highly sensitive to external tuning parameters such as chemical substitution and hydrostatic pressure, which generally suppress long-range order. Here, using high-resolution X-ray diffraction under controlled uniaxial strain, we show that anisotropic lattice deformation instead stabilizes and enhances the CDW state in ScV$_6$Sn$_6$. Compression along the [H00] and [HH0] directions lowers the crystal symmetry from hexagonal to orthorhombic, lifts the degeneracy between symmetry-equivalent in-plane CDW domains, and promotes long-range order while preserving the underlying trimer instability. Phonon calculations indicate only a moderate stabilization of the imaginary flat phonon mode, demonstrating that the increase in T$_\mathrm{CDW}$ is primarily driven by the in-plane ordering of the Sn$^\mathrm{T}$--Sc--Sn$^\mathrm{T}$ \textit{rattling} chains within the frustrated kagome lattice. A phenomenological model incorporating strain-dependent Ising couplings within a three-state Potts framework successfully reproduces the evolution of T$_\mathrm{CDW}$ under compression and captures the continuous nature of the transition. Our results establish uniaxial strain as a powerful symmetry-selective tuning parameter for order-disorder transformations in frustrated lattices.
summary: This work demonstrates that applying controlled uniaxial strain can selectively stabilize and enhance Charge-Density Wave order in $	ext{ScV}_6	ext{Sn}_6$. By inducing a symmetry-lowering orthorhombic distortion, the researchers show that the CDW transition temperature increases significantly. The findings combine advanced X-ray diffraction measurements with sophisticated Potts model simulations to provide a powerful tuning mechanism for frustrated quantum materials.
main_problem: The study investigates how anisotropic lattice deformation can selectively stabilize and enhance Charge-Density Wave (CDW) order in frustrated kagome metals, contrasting with general suppression mechanisms.
main_result: Uniaxial strain acts as a powerful symmetry-selective tuning parameter, stabilizing the CDW state by inducing an orthorhombic distortion that lifts domain degeneracy. The enhancement is attributed to the ordering of rattling chains within the lattice structure.
method: The research combines high-resolution X-ray diffraction under controlled uniaxial strain (experiment) with phenomenological modeling using a three-state Potts framework and Monte Carlo simulations (theory).
model_or_system: The physical system is $	ext{ScV}_6	ext{Sn}_6$, a kagome metal exhibiting CDW order. The underlying physics is modeled by mapping the low-temperature state onto an effective three-state Potts model incorporating strain-dependent Ising couplings.
key_observables: CDW transition temperature ($T_{	ext{CDW}}$), lattice parameters ($	ext{a}_o, 	ext{b}_o, 	ext{c}_o$), diffuse scattering intensity, and the evolution of structural symmetry from hexagonal to orthorhombic.
important_parameters: Uniaxial strain magnitude (e.g., $-0.6\%$ compression along $[	ext{HH}0]$), temperature ($T_{	ext{CDW}}$), and coupling constants within the Potts model.
main_assumptions: The CDW physics can be effectively mapped onto a three-state Potts model, and the enhancement mechanism is primarily driven by anisotropic lattice deformation breaking rotational symmetry.
figures_summary: Figures detail structural transformations from hexagonal to orthorhombic phases under strain; simulations show $T_{	ext{CDW}}$ increasing monotonically with compressive strain; ARPES confirms electronic renormalization localized at specific terminations ($	ext{V}_3	ext{Sn}$).
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the structural consequences of uniaxial strain (XRD/structural analysis), then modeling the resulting order-disorder transition using a Potts model (MC simulations), and finally confirming the electronic signature via ARPES.
why_interesting: While focused on CDW physics, the use of symmetry breaking via external fields (strain) to tune an intrinsic many-body instability is highly relevant to controlling quantum phases in condensed matter systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (A) High-temperature crystal structure (P6/mmm). V atoms (red) form a kagome lattice, SnH atoms (light grey) occupy honeycomb sites, and SnT (blue) and Sc (purple) form trimers along the out-of-plane direction. (B) CDW-induced structural modulation with the SnT–Sc–SnT

Figure 2. (A) View of the crystal structure along the c direction, showing three merohedral orthorhombic domains. Arrows indicate the reciprocal-lattice directions along which uniaxial compressive strain was applied. (B) Upper: sketch of the atomic displacements after the application of uniaxial strain along [H00] direction. Dashed lines indicate the undistorted structure; the final orthorhombic lattice is indicated by solid black lines. Bottom: Sketch of the uniaxial strain device. (C) Shift of characteristic Bragg peaks under applied strain. (D) Evolution of the orthorhombic lattice parameters and volume as a function of applied strain along the [H00] direction measured at T = 107 K. (E)...

Figure 3. (A–C) Atomic projection of the phonon dispersion of ScV6Sn6 for the (A) no-strain case, (B) 1.5% compression along [H00], and (C) 1.5% compression along the [HH0] directions, respectively. The color code describes the different atomic weight on each phonon mode.

Figure 4. (A) Sketch of the real space definition of the cou- pling strengths, Ji, accounting for the first and second near- est neighbors in the orthorhombic cell. ah and b identify the hexagonal reciprocal lattice vectors. (B) Strain dependence of the transition temperature, TCDW, extracted from the in- tegrated peak area in the Monte Carlo simulation.
